dMRV (Digital Measurement, Reporting, and Verification)

What is dMRV and Why Should You Care?

Section titled “What is dMRV and Why Should You Care?”

Imagine you plant a tree today. How do you prove it grew? How do you show the world that your community’s hard work is making a real difference? This is where dMRV comes in.

dMRV stands for Digital Measurement, Reporting, and Verification. It sounds technical, but let’s break it down simply:

  • Measurement = Counting or recording what happened (like how many trees were planted)
  • Reporting = Sharing that information with others
  • Verification = Proving that the information is true

The “digital” part just means we use phones and computers to do this faster and more reliably.

Why Does This Matter for Your Work?

Section titled “Why Does This Matter for Your Work?”

When you work on regenerative projects—like planting trees, managing water sources, or protecting grazing lands—you need to show the impact you’re making. Here’s why dMRV matters:

1. Your Work Gets Recognized

Section titled “1. Your Work Gets Recognized”

When you can prove the results of your work, organizations and funders can see the real change happening in your community. This means more support and resources for future projects.

2. Building Trust

Section titled “2. Building Trust”

Verification proves that your community’s efforts are genuine. It’s like having a witness who confirms: “Yes, these women really did plant 500 trees this season.”

3. Access to New Opportunities

Section titled “3. Access to New Opportunities”

Many carbon markets and environmental programs now require verified impact data. Learning dMRV opens doors to these opportunities.

Real-World Examples You Can Understand

Section titled “Real-World Examples You Can Understand”

Example 1: The Tree Planting Project

Section titled “Example 1: The Tree Planting Project”

Fatima’s group in Hargeisa planted 200 tree seedlings last rainy season. With dMRV, they used a simple app to:

  • Take photos of each seedling location
  • Record survival rates after 3 months
  • Report the data to their partner organization

Now they have proof of their impact, which helped them get funding for more seedlings.

Example 2: Water Conservation

Section titled “Example 2: Water Conservation”

A women’s cooperative in Berbera tracked water levels in their local catchments using simple digital forms. The verified data helped them negotiate for better water infrastructure from the local government.

Example 3: Grazing Land Recovery

Section titled “Example 3: Grazing Land Recovery”

By documenting grass regrowth in previously degraded areas, community members proved that their rest-rotation grazing was working. Satellite verification confirmed their observations.

How Does dMRV Work? A Simple Breakdown

Section titled “How Does dMRV Work? A Simple Breakdown”

Here’s what a typical dMRV process looks like:

  1. Collect data — Use a phone app or simple form to record what you observe (photos, numbers, locations)

  2. Submit reports — Send the information to a central system or organization

  3. Get verified — Either automated systems (like satellite imagery) or human verifiers check that your data is accurate

  4. Receive confirmation — You get documentation proving your impact

Getting Started: Your First Steps

Section titled “Getting Started: Your First Steps”

You don’t need expensive equipment or advanced training. Here’s how to begin:

Step 1: Start Simple

Section titled “Step 1: Start Simple”

Begin by taking photos of your project activities with your phone. Note the date, location, and what you’re documenting.

Step 2: Use What You Have

Section titled “Step 2: Use What You Have”

Many dMRV tools work on basic smartphones. Apps like KoboToolbox, ODK, or even simple Google Forms can work for data collection.

Step 3: Learn the Basics

Section titled “Step 3: Learn the Basics”

Ask your program coordinators about any dMRV training opportunities. Many organizations offer free workshops.

Step 4: Build the Habit

Section titled “Step 4: Build the Habit”

Make data collection part of your project routine. A few minutes each week adds up to powerful evidence over time.

Step 5: Connect with Others

Section titled “Step 5: Connect with Others”

Join networks of other practitioners. You can share tips and learn from communities already using dMRV.

Tips for Success

Section titled “Tips for Success”
  • Be consistent — Regular data collection is more valuable than occasional detailed records
  • Photo evidence is powerful — Before/after photos are some of the most convincing verification
  • Keep records simple — Don’t overcomplicate; start with basic tracking and expand later
  • Ask for help — Your program coordinators and mentors are there to support you

What dMRV Enables

Section titled “What dMRV Enables”

When your community uses dMRV, you become the storytellers of your own impact. You’re not just doing the work—you’re documenting the change. This means:

  • Your voice matters — The data you collect tells your story in numbers
  • Your impact is visible — Decision-makers can see what’s happening on the ground
  • Your future is brighter — Verified impact opens doors to funding, partnerships, and recognition

Moving Forward

Section titled “Moving Forward”

dMRV might feel new, but remember: you’ve already been measuring and reporting your work in your heads for years. You’re simply finding new ways to capture and share that knowledge.

Start small. Be consistent. Trust the process.

Your community’s work deserves to be recognized—and dMRV helps make that happen.

Next Steps:

  • Talk to your program coordinator about dMRV tools available to you
  • Practice taking structured photos of your project activities
  • Keep a simple log of what you do each week

You’ve got this! 🌱
